changes.reason: Lower entry bar to capture more trades. Zero losses suggests we have room to be less selective. A 0.5 threshold should increase trade frequency while still requiring meaningfully positive sentiment.

With a 100% win rate, winners are reaching the 3x target consistently. Widening to 3.5x lets winners run further. If win rate drops after loosening the entry threshold, this compensates by extracting more from each winner.
Modest increase from 10% to 12% per position. The strong win rate justifies slightly more capital per trade to amplify the proven edge, while still keeping risk manageable.

With only 3 closed trades, edge is unproven. Reduce max position size from 12% to 10% (the config default) to limit downside on the inevitable first loss while the strategy builds a larger sample. Confidence-scaled sizing already reduces actual size on low-confidence entries, but capping the max adds a safety margin.
Filter out marginal sentiment signals like the MSFT trade (0.553 barely passed). Raising to 0.55 would have blocked that losing trade while still capturing the stronger TSLA setup (0.669).
MSFT entered at RSI 68.9, just under the 70 cutoff, and went nowhere. Lowering to 65 blocks buys into already-extended momentum, which would have filtered this trade.
With only 5 trades, sample is small but a wider stop gives winners more room to develop. Moving from 2.0 to 2.5 ATR reduces noise-driven stop-outs while keeping risk bounded.
With tighter entry filters, fewer trades will trigger. Slightly reducing position size to 8% adds a risk buffer while the stricter parameters are validated over more trades.

Both recent trades had sentiment barely above threshold (0.635, 0.669). Raising to 0.62 filters out the weakest signals while still allowing these trades — but the confidence calc's excess-sentiment component (sentiment - threshold) * 2 will produce higher confidence scores, leading to better position sizing and fewer marginal entries.

RSI overbought at 65 is too restrictive — it blocks entries in healthy uptrends. The confidence calc already penalizes RSI above 60 by giving 0 RSI-health bonus. Widening to 70 lets momentum trades through while the confidence mechanism naturally sizes them smaller.

With 4 losses in 7 trades and flat exits on recent trades, stops may be too tight. Widening from 2.4 to 2.8 ATR gives trades more room to breathe, reducing whipsaw stops. Combined with the higher sentiment threshold filtering out weaker setups, the wider stop is applied only to higher-conviction trades.

With the wider stop (2.8), maintaining reward/risk ratio requires raising TP to 4.0 (ratio 1.43:1, similar to current 1.46:1). Since avg P&L is positive, winners can run further. This also compensates for fewer trades from the higher sentiment threshold.

Slight reduction in max position size as a risk management measure while the other parameter changes settle in. With only 7 trades of data, we should be conservative with sizing until the adjusted parameters prove out over more trades.

Filter out marginal sentiment entries. The TSLA trade (sentiment 0.77) would still pass, but weaker signals that contributed to the 5 losses get filtered. This should meaningfully improve win rate by only entering on stronger conviction.

5 of 8 trades were losses, suggesting premature stop-outs. Widening from 2.8x to 3.2x ATR gives trades more room to breathe through normal volatility, reducing the chance of being stopped out before the thesis plays out.

Slightly tighter take-profit to convert more trades into wins. Current 4.0x target may be too ambitious given the win rate. At 3.6x with a 3.2x stop, the reward-risk ratio is still favorable (1.125:1) and the higher win rate should preserve positive expectancy.

Slight reduction in position size as a risk management measure while the other parameter changes are validated. With only 8 trades of data, reducing exposure per trade limits downside during the calibration period.

Filter out lower-conviction entries. MSFT entered at 0.706 sentiment with only 0.452 confidence — barely above threshold. Simulation winners clustered at higher sentiment scores. A small raise to 0.72 would have blocked that marginal MSFT entry while still capturing strong signals like TSLA (1.0 sentiment).

The SL multiplier was widened from default 2.0 to 3.2 — too aggressive. This makes stops very wide, increasing loss size when hit. Tightening to 2.5 (still above default) reduces per-trade risk while giving enough room to avoid noise. Combined with the TP change below, this restores a 1.6:1 reward:risk ratio.

Slightly widen take-profit to let winners run more. Simulation data showed TSLA winners hitting TP cleanly with the old 3.0 multiplier on high-ATR names. With the tighter stop-loss, a 4.0 TP multiplier gives a 1.6:1 reward:risk ratio — closer to the original 1.5:1 design that produced +5% sim returns.

With only 8 closed trades, slightly reducing position size is prudent risk management during the live validation phase. This limits downside per trade while the other parameter changes take effect. Can increase back to 0.06 once win rate stabilizes above 35%.

70% of trades lose despite a 0.72 threshold. Raising to 0.78 filters out weaker sentiment signals that likely lack conviction. This should improve win rate at the cost of fewer trades, which is acceptable given the positive avg P&L structure.

Slight widening to reduce stop-outs from normal volatility. With 7 losses out of 10, some stops may be getting clipped by noise before the trade thesis plays out. A modest increase stays within the 20% governor cap.

Slightly tightening TP to capture more winners. With only 3 wins out of 10, some trades may be reversing before hitting the 4.0x target. Reducing to 3.6x should convert near-misses into realized wins while maintaining a favorable 1.29:1 reward-risk ratio (3.6/2.8).
Filter out low-conviction entries. The MSFT trade had 0.80 sentiment and only 0.44 confidence — raising the bar should eliminate marginal setups and improve win rate.
With a 30% win rate but positive expectancy, winners must compensate for frequent losers. Widening take-profit lets winners run further, increasing average win size.
Reduce per-trade risk while win rate is low. Once win rate improves above 40%, this can be raised back.

Raise selectivity to filter out low-conviction trades like the TSLA entry (sentiment 1.0 but confidence only 0.56). Combined with confidence, this reduces entries that lack multiple confirming signals.

9 of 12 trades lost, suggesting frequent stop-outs from normal volatility. Widening stops from 2.8x to 3.2x ATR gives trades more room to breathe, reducing false stop-outs while the higher entry threshold limits exposure to only high-conviction setups.

With a 25% win rate and positive P&L, winners are large but rare. Slightly tightening take-profit from 4.0x to 3.6x ATR should capture more winning trades before they reverse, improving win rate while maintaining favorable reward:risk (3.6/3.2 = 1.125:1 minimum).
With fewer but higher-conviction trades, slightly reducing position size to 3.5% adds a risk buffer during the parameter adjustment period. Can be revisited upward once win rate improves.
0.88 is too restrictive — zero trades in 7 days. Lowering to 0.78 increases opportunity flow while still requiring strong bullish sentiment. Capped at ~11% change.
With 25% win rate, winners must be large. Widening take-profit from 3.6 to 4.3 ATR improves reward/risk ratio from 1.13:1 to 1.34:1, letting winners run further. ~19% change.
Tightening stop-loss slightly reduces loss size per trade and improves effective R:R ratio to 1.54:1 (4.3/2.8). The current 3.2x is generous — 2.8x still gives room to avoid noise. ~12.5% change.
At 3.5%, winners are capped too small. Increasing to 4.2% lets profitable trades contribute more to P&L while staying conservative. ~20% change.
